content:
4 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

AI can finish a task in minutes. The result still has to be understood, checked, connected to something that matters, and acted on. That part rarely appears in the demonstration. The screen shows a completed answer, so the work looks complete.
The gap becomes harder to ignore once AI moves beyond a single conversation. An agent can keep working while I am doing something else. Several agents can run in parallel, each returning useful material on its own schedule. Their capacity to produce keeps growing. My capacity to absorb what they produce still has the same number of hours behind it.
Every output arrives with a decision attached. Can I trust this enough to use it? What should change because I know it? When those decisions remain open, faster production creates a larger queue.
I learned this by building a system that worked.
The system kept getting better
Over the past several months, I have been building my OpenClaw setup, a group of AI agents running on a Mac Mini. The agents collect information, organize it, and create work I can use. They run on schedules, maintain memory files, and feed a personal wiki that carries information from one session to the next.
The system has produced real value. Some of its work has made its way into content I have shared. Building it has also taught me how agentic systems behave in a way that reading about them never could. I can see how context moves through a system, where an agent loses the thread, and how a small design choice changes what comes back later.
That progress made expansion easy to justify. I added more agents and gave them more inputs. I improved the way they remembered prior work and connected their outputs. Each addition made the system more capable. It also made the system harder for me to hold in my head.
Several agents were now working in parallel on different jobs. Before I could use an output, I sometimes had to refresh my understanding of the design around it. I needed to remember what the agent had seen, how its job differed from another agent’s, and where the result was supposed to go next. I kept diagrams and design notes because the architecture was changing faster than my memory of it.
The system could complete a run without that context. I could not make a confident decision from the result without rebuilding some of it first.
The queue belonged to me
The morning brief made the mismatch visible. Each day the system brought me a development worth understanding. A useful brief might connect to something already in the wiki and open several directions I wanted to explore.
The next morning, another one arrived.
The individual brief was rarely the problem. The rate was. Yesterday’s idea could still be waiting for me to digest it while today’s was already asking for the same attention. The agent started each run with a clean task. I carried the unfinished queue from every run before it.
Useful information made this harder, not easier. Noise is simple to discard. A good connection feels like an obligation. Once the system found something that might improve a decision or become part of future work, ignoring it felt wasteful. The wiki preserved the idea, but it also preserved my sense that I should eventually do something with it.
Meanwhile, improving OpenClaw remained concrete and satisfying. I could adjust a prompt or redesign a handoff and see the result. Those changes looked like progress because they were progress. They just happened on the supply side of a system whose constraint had moved somewhere else.
My inputs and the system’s internal output were growing much faster than the outcomes I could trace back to them. I had made production cheap. Judgment still took my time, and the growing architecture added a coordination cost before that judgment could even begin.
I was missing a conversion mechanism
I had designed the agents around the work they should complete. I had paid less attention to what I would do after they completed it.
That handoff now matters more to me than adding another capability. An agent’s job is incomplete without a clear downstream purpose. Its output should support a current decision or move active work forward. If the result has no destination, storing it more carefully only delays the same decision.
I am still working out what this looks like in practice. One change I am testing is requiring a small act of judgment at a defined review point. It may be only one sentence recording why a brief matters and whether it connects to current work. That keeps digestion incremental instead of pushing all of it into an undefined future session.
The same test applies before I add another agent. I need to know what human decision it will support and how often I can realistically review its work. Information that does not connect to current work can remain in the archive without repeatedly returning to my attention.
I am also changing how I judge the system. A completed run tells me that the machinery worked. The more useful measure is whether the result changed what I did or removed work that would otherwise have remained with me. A new agent has to justify the continuing attention required to understand and use what it produces.
This mechanism will keep evolving because my uses for AI will keep evolving. I do not expect one workflow to settle the problem permanently. The goal is to keep the distance between production and action from expanding unnoticed.
The next version of OpenClaw may produce less. If I can understand more of what it produces and convert a larger share into useful action, that will be an improvement.
What happens to the work your AI produces after it reaches you?
